senses.definition: the natural agent that stimulates sight and makes things visible
examples: the light of the sun
: the street lamps shed a faint light into the room

senses.definition: a source of illumination, especially an electric lamp
examples: a light came on in his room

senses.definition: decorative illuminations
examples: Christmas lights

senses.definition: a traffic light
examples: turn right at the light

senses.definition: the amount or quality of light in a place
examples: the plant requires good light
in some lights she could look beautiful

senses.definition: an expression in someone's eyes indicating a particular emotion or mood
examples: a shrewd light entered his eyes

senses.definition: a person's opinions, standards, and abilities
examples: leaving the police to do the job according to their lights

senses.definition: understanding of a problem or mystery; enlightenment
examples: she saw light dawn on the woman's face

senses.definition: spiritual illumination by divine truth.
an area of something that is brighter or paler than its surroundings
examples: sunshine will brighten the natural lights in your hair

senses.definition: a device that makes something start burning, as a match, lighter, or flame
examples: he asked me for a light

senses.definition: a window or opening in a wall to let light in
examples: the bedroom has a wide bay with leaded lights

senses.definition: any of the perpendicular divisions of a mullioned window.
any of the panes of glass forming the roof or side of a greenhouse or the top of a cold frame.
a person notable or eminent in a particular sphere of activity or place
examples: volunteers have been the shining lights of the community

senses.definition: provide with light or lighting; illuminate
examples: the room was lighted by a number of small lamps

senses.definition: switch on (an electric light)
examples: only one of the table lamps was lit

senses.definition: make (something) start burning; ignite
examples: Allen gathered sticks and lit a fire

senses.definition: begin to burn; be ignited
examples: the gas wouldn't light properly

senses.definition: having a considerable or sufficient amount of natural light; not dark
examples: the bedrooms are light and airy
it was almost light outside

senses.definition: pale
examples: her eyes were light blue

notes.content: Old English lēoht, līht (noun and adjective), līhtan (verb), of Germanic origin; related to Dutch licht and German Licht, from an Indo-European root shared by Greek leukos ‘white’ and Latin lux ‘light’.

examples: an investigation to bring to light examples of extravagant expenditure
no new facts came to light

items.definition: fall asleep or lose consciousness suddenly
examples: she returned to bed and went out like a light

items.definition: in the way specified; so as to give a specified impression
examples: the audit portrayed the company in a very favorable light

items.definition: a long-awaited indication that a period of hardship or adversity is nearing an end
examples: it had been a hard struggle but I could see a glimmer of light at the end of the tunnel
items.phrase: light at the end of the tunnel

examples: we sailed at the first light of day
bringing old family secrets into the light of day
she was his only child, the light of his life

items.definition: bedtime in a school dormitory, military barracks, or other institution, when lights should be switched off
examples: a few minutes before lights out

items.definition: understand or realize something after prolonged thought or doubt.

examples: this software first saw the light of day back in 1993

items.definition: become bright on account of a light being shone or switched on; become illuminated
examples: the sign to fasten seat belts lit up
lightning suddenly lit up the house
his eyes lit up and he smiled
a smile of delight lit up her face
workers who light up in prohibited areas face dismissal
she shrugged and lit up another cigarette
the Tigers were lit up by the Giants in Game 1

phraseGroups.rawContent: lightish | ˈlīdiSH | adjective lightless | ˈlītləs | adjective
phraseGroups.title: DERIVATIVES
